_meta.note: Summarized model-code and Florida-amendment excerpts for quick field reference. NOT a substitute for the authoritative adopted code — always verify with AHJ before permit, install, or inspection.

editions.FBC-Mechanical: 2020 (7th Edition, based on 2018 IMC with FL amendments)

_meta.disclaimer: Informational summary only. Code text is paraphrased. Verify against the authoritative adopted code and local amendments with your AHJ.

entries.summary: Appliances shall be installed with manufacturer-specified clearances from combustibles and sufficient service access. Where listing and instructions differ from table values, the listing controls.
gotchas: A 30" wide × 30" deep service clearance in front of the control side is common — confirm on the appliance nameplate.
Working space at electrical panels (NEC 110.26) applies when panel shares the equipment space — don't block with a furnace.
Attic installation requires permanent lighting and a receptacle at the appliance per IMC 306.

entries.summary: Attic appliances require a 22" × 30" passageway (max 20 ft), a 30" × 30" service platform in front of the access side, permanent lighting at the appliance, and a 120V receptacle within 25 ft on the same side of the access.
gotchas: Solid flooring from the attic access to the appliance is required where the passageway is over insulation.
Passageway max length can be reduced if walking is required on the framing only.
GFCI required for the attic receptacle (NEC 210.8).

entries.summary: Condensate from cooling coils shall be disposed of via a primary drain sized per coil capacity (¾" nominal min for most residential). A secondary means (secondary pan w/ float switch, secondary drain line, or auxiliary float switch on primary line) is required for units installed above ceilings or in locations where overflow causes damage.
gotchas: P-trap must match the blower static — negative static pulls air through a shallow trap.
Never tie condensate to a sanitary drain unless properly trapped and air-gapped.
Secondary drain must terminate to a conspicuous location so the owner notices the overflow.

entries.topic: mechanical ventilation rates and outdoor air
entries.summary: Residential dwelling units require whole-house mechanical ventilation per ASHRAE 62.2 (as adopted). Rate ≈ 7.5 CFM per occupant + 3 CFM per 100 ft² of floor area. Commercial spaces use Table 403.3.1.1.
gotchas: Exhaust-only works but depressurizes the home — risk for backdrafting atmospheric appliances.
Balanced ventilation (ERV/HRV) preferred in humid climates.
Don't count intermittent bath exhaust toward whole-house rate unless it runs on a schedule.

entries.topic: exhaust systems general, dryer duct, kitchen hood
entries.summary: Exhaust systems shall discharge to the outside with a backdraft damper and minimum clearances from openings. Kitchen hoods > 400 CFM require makeup air (IMC 501.4.1). Dryer exhaust: rigid metal, max 35 ft equivalent length (reduce per elbows per IRC M1502).
gotchas: Flex transition duct allowed only between rigid duct and the dryer — UL 2158A rated.
Range-hood termination cannot recirculate into the attic or soffit.
Bath fan must terminate outside, not into an attic or between joists.

entries.topic: plenums, return air, duct construction, insulation
entries.summary: Plenums shall not contain storage or combustibles. Ducts outside conditioned space require R-8 minimum (climate zones 2-8) or R-6 for supply/return in conditioned space per IECC. Flex duct supports: every 4 ft max, max sag ½" per foot.
gotchas: Fuel-gas lines inside plenums must be welded/brazed (no flex connectors).
Return air cannot be taken from garages, under-floor crawl spaces, or mechanical rooms containing fuel appliances.
Duct leakage testing required at rough and final in most adopted IECC editions.

entries.topic: refrigerant piping, line set burial, brazing, leak test
entries.summary: Refrigerant piping shall be brazed with dry nitrogen purge. Line sets below grade must be protected from corrosion and physical damage (conduit/sleeve). Post-brazing: pressure test + evacuate to 500 microns before charging.
gotchas: No-nitrogen brazing is the #1 cause of TXV/compressor contamination.
Evacuation stays below 500µ for 10 min with vac pump valved off — rising indicates moisture or leak.
A2L refrigerants (R-32, R-454B) require compliant fittings and worker training per IMC 1106.

entries.summary: Dryer exhaust duct: 4" nominal, rigid metal, smooth interior, max equivalent length 35 ft. Deduct for each 90° elbow (typ 5 ft) and 45° (typ 2.5 ft). Manufacturer may allow longer with booster fan.
gotchas: Plastic or vinyl duct is NEVER allowed.
Screws protruding into the duct catch lint — use foil tape or crimp joints with the male end pointing downstream.
Termination backdraft damper and no bird screen smaller than ¼".

entries.topic: water supply pipe sizing, fixture units
entries.summary: Water supply pipe sized per WSFU (water supply fixture units). Table 604.3 lists WSFU per fixture type. Pipe size selected from flow/pressure/length via Tables 604.10.1 and Hunter's curve.
gotchas: Tankless water heaters have a dedicated flow rate requirement — check manufacturer sizing chart, not just WSFU.
PEX effective ID is smaller than copper of same nominal size — use PEX-specific sizing tables.
Max flow velocity typically 8 fps hot, 10 fps cold (higher erodes pipe).

entries.topic: DWV fixture units and drain sizing
entries.summary: Drainage fixture units (DFU) assigned per Table 709.1 (water closet=4, lavatory=1, tub=2, kitchen sink=2, shower=2, etc.). Pipe sizes selected from Table 710.1 by DFU load and slope.
gotchas: Minimum slope: ⅛"/ft for 3" and larger; ¼"/ft for 2" and smaller.
Water closet drain minimum 3" for a single WC; 4" if serving more than 6 WCs on same branch.
Cleanouts at every change of direction > 45°, max 100 ft apart on horizontal runs ≥ 4".

entries.summary: Vent pipe sized ≥ ½ drain pipe size (but not less than 1¼"). Stack vent / vent stack terminates min 6" above roof, min 10 ft horizontally from any window/door or 3 ft above.
gotchas: AAVs permitted for island sinks and remote fixtures only where local AHJ accepts — not universally allowed.
Vent termination below adjacent window opening is an auto-fail on inspection.
Frost closure: cold climates require 3" vent through roof to resist icing.

entries.summary: In Miami-Dade and Broward counties (HVHZ), all mechanical products exposed to wind uplift require Miami-Dade Notice of Acceptance (NOA) or FL Product Approval. Outdoor units must be anchored to resist design wind per FBC (140+ mph).
gotchas: Line-set penetrations through exterior walls require NOA-approved flashing/sealants.
Condenser tie-down straps/anchors must be part of the approved assembly — not aftermarket.
Verify product NOA number on the ASCE 7 wind-load calc submitted with permit.

entries.topic: outdoor unit anchoring and wind loading
entries.summary: Outdoor condensing units in Florida must be anchored to a concrete pad or approved composite pad with straps rated for the design wind speed (typ 140 mph outside HVHZ, 175+ mph in HVHZ).
gotchas: 4" concrete pad with #3 rebar grid is common; verify engineering per local AHJ.
Raised platforms in flood zones must be above BFE (Base Flood Elevation).
Strap bolts into composite pads require manufacturer-specified embedment depth.
entries.source: 2020 FBC-Mechanical and ASCE 7 wind load provisions.

entries.topic: condensate drain discharge and routing in Florida
entries.summary: Condensate may discharge to a properly sized exterior location (splash block, yard, dry well) or to an indirect waste. Florida-specific: never to a sanitary sewer without approved air gap; never into swimming pool return.
gotchas: Long horizontal condensate runs in unconditioned attics must be insulated to prevent sweating.
In HVHZ, condensate termination point must be secured against hurricane disturbance.
Biofilm/algae clogs are the #1 service call in FL summers — add in-line algae tablets and cleanout access.
